original_abstract: Communication history in socialist Europe has long been defined by its association with state propaganda. Following its inception, the USSR established itself as the world’s first “propaganda state”—a term coined by Peter Kenez to describe a radical new communications order that would eventually be emulated across the Eastern Bloc. In his seminal 1985 study, Kenez argued that the Bolshevik regime was unique not just for its ideological ambitions, but for its systematic attempt to utilize political education to forge a "new humanity" capable of inhabiting a transformed society. Under this framework, propaganda was not merely a tool for messaging but a foundational instrument of social engineering.
humanized_abstract: Communication history in socialist Europe is associated largely with propaganda. The USSR became the world’s first propaganda state in the sense of Peter Kenez’s expression describing a radically new system of communication which was later copied by other countries in Eastern Europe. Kenez published his classic study on The Birth of the Propaganda Machine. Studies in the Technique of Bolshevik Conquest (1985), in which he described how the Bolsheviks were different, and in which they tried to create a ‘new man’ in order to establish a new society using ‘political education’. Propaganda in the Soviet case was a mechanism to carry out this aim in societal transformation.

Propaganda in the Soviet case was a mechanism to carry out this aim in societal transformation.
